coredata.dc:title: A real-time and highly sensitive fiber optic biosensor based on the carbon quantum dots for nitric oxide detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Wei
Huang, Jun
Ding, Liyun
Lin, Haitao
Yu, Sha
Yuan, Fang
Liang, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Nitric oxide (NO) is a signal molecule with a wide range of functions and unique properties in organisms. A novel fluorescent probe composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with o-phenylenediamine groups was prepared by the microwave method to detect NO selectively. The mechanism of the reaction between CQDs and NO was established by a series of characterization methods, such as fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer and transmission electron microscope. The result showed that the o-phenylenediamine groups on the surface of the CQDs can react with NO to form an electron-free triazole structure, leading to the decrease of fluorescence of the CQDs. Furthermore, a new optical fiber sensing system based on the CQDs was constructed to detect NO with a low detection limit of 9.12 nM at laser power of 280 mW. In addition, the NO optic fiber biosensor also was used to detect NO concentration in real human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitric oxide sensing
Carbon quantum dots
Fiber optic biosensor
Fluorescence quenching
Real-time detection
serum detection

Construction of optical sensing system for nitric oxide detection
Structure and properties of the CQDs
Mechanism analysis of NO detection by the CQDs
Calibration tests of the NO optic fiber sensor
Comparison of the sensor and NO assay kit
Detection of NO in human serum

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The experimental device diagram of NO optical fiber biosensor.

(a)The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. (b)XRD pattern of the CQDs. (c) TEM image of the CQDs. (d) Cytotoxic effects on SH-SY5Y cells were cultured for 24 h and 48 h by adding different amounts of CQDs.
(a) SEM image of the membrane coated on fiber end face. (b) AFM image of the membrane coated on fiber end face.
The fluorescence intensity of CQDs after adding different concentrations of NO.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the CQDs before and after adding NO. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of the CQDs in the absence and presence of NO. (c) XPS analysis of N1s before addition of NO. (d) XPS analysis of N1s after addition of NO.

(a) Sequence chart of standard NO solution detection by optical fiber sensor. Calibration curve of the optical fiber sensor: (b) Calibration of NO concentration at μM level; (c) Calibration of NO concentration at nM level. (d) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the sensor in the presence of 50 μM NO solution and 10 mM different substances, respectively.
Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching of the CQDs by NO.
The main element content of CQDs before and after the addition of NO.
Comparison of detection accuracy between NO kit and the optical fiber sensor.

row.entry: Standard deviation of NO assay kit
Standard deviation of the optical fiber sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The experimental results of detecting the recovery rate by adding standard NO solution to the serum.

row.entry: Mixed serum NO calculated by formula (μM)

head.ce:title: A real-time and highly sensitive fiber optic biosensor based on the carbon quantum dots for nitric oxide detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Fiber Optic Sensing Technology, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Fiber Optic Sensing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Fiber Optic Sensing Technology, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Health, Qilu Hospital, Shandong University, Qingdao 266035, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Health, Qilu Hospital, Shandong University, Qingdao 266035, China;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Neurology, Qilu Hospital, Shandong University, Qingdao 266035, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Neurology, Qilu Hospital, Shandong University, Qingdao 266035, China;

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitric oxide (NO) is a signal molecule with a wide range of functions and unique properties in organisms. A novel fluorescent probe composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with o-phenylenediamine groups was prepared by the microwave method to detect NO selectively. The mechanism of the reaction between CQDs and NO was established by a series of characterization methods, such as fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer and transmission electron microscope. The result showed that the o-phenylenediamine groups on the surface of the CQDs can react with NO to form an electron-free triazole structure, leading to the decrease of fluorescence of the CQDs. Furthermore, a new optical fiber sensing system based on the CQDs was constructed to detect NO with a low detection limit of 9.12 nM at laser power of 280 mW. In addition, the NO optic fiber biosensor also was used to detect NO concentration in real human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO) is an important biological messenger molecule that plays a unique physiological role in life. It is widely involved in human nerve conduction, vasodilation and immune processes, greatly affecting the normal functioning of physiological functions [1–3]. However, it is very difficult to accurately and real-time detect the concentration of NO in biological samples. The reason is that the concentration of NO in biological samples is very low with nmol/L level, and NO is a small free-radical gas with short half-life [4]. Abnormal NO generation was known to be responsible for serious chronic diseases, including neurodegenerative injury and inflammatory bowel disease [5,6]. Therefore, it is desirable to exploit an accurate and sensitive method for detecting NO effectively in complex biological systems. Researchers have established many analytical methods on NO detection such as ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy [7], chemiluminescence [8], electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy [9], electrochemical methods [10,11], chromatography [12,13], capillary electrophoresis [14], and fluorescence analysis [15–17]. Among them, the fluorescence analysis has the advantages of high sensitivity and good selectivity. Fluorescence analysis mainly uses fluorescent probe to react with NO to produce compounds with different optical properties from the unreacted, original fluorescent probe. The fluorescent properties of fluorescent probe can be used to detect NO quantitatively [18].

There are many NO fluorescent probes reported so far, including ruthenium porphyrin complexes, [19] π-conjugated polymer [20], substituted 2-(2-aminophenyl) benzothiazole [21], rhodamine deoxy lactam [22], which all have certain toxicity, and the synthesis process is very complex, leading to certain limitations in the actual detection process. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm and good photoluminescence properties [23,24]. It has excellent properties different from traditional fluorescent probes, such as good water solubility, chemical inertia, low toxicity, easy functionalization, photobleaching resistance and photostability. Thus, CQDs can be modified to be used as a fluorescent probe in the field of biological NO detection [25–28].

However, real-time quantitative measurement of NO in biological systems remains a challenge on account of its short half-life and rapid diffusion in organisms. At present, the most analytical methods for NO detection are unavailable for in vivo detection with a long sample pretreatment process [29]. There are some defects in the method of detecting NO by fluorescence quenching reported in the previous literature [30]. They are completely detected in the environment of aqueous solution, and the fluorescence can be stable only after the fluorescence probe diffuses uniformly in the solution to be measured, so the detection time is longer. In addition, the detection process is completely dependent on the fluorescence spectrometer, so the effect of real-time on-line monitoring cannot be realized. We proposed a real-time on-line detection method for NO combining the advantages of optical fiber sensing and fluorescence quenching, which has not been seen before. Optical fiber sensing technology has great advantages in the development of real-time and rapid sensing technology. It is a kind of advanced sensing technology that uses photosensitive components or optical sensitive materials to convert the target parameters to corresponding optical signals and collect and transmit them through optical fiber. The optical fiber sensor consists of four parts: optical transmitter, optical fiber, sensing element and signal detector. Its transmission capacity is large, and the information dimension reflected in the output is diversified. It can make full use of phase, polarization, wavelength offset, attenuation distribution to distinguish the signal, and realize the sensing detection of specific substances in complex system by analyzing multi spectral data. Optic fiber biosensor has many advantages such as small sample volume, high in sensitivity and resisting strong electromagnetic interference, especially entitling to a real-time and highly sensitive detection for NO in vivo and in vitro, so it provides many possibilities in the future medical detection field.

In this paper, we combined the advantages of fluorescence analysis and optical fiber sensing to build a new biosensor for NO detection. We prepared CQDs with NO recognition groups on the surface by microwave reaction as fluorescent probe for NO detection. Additionally, a real-time and highly sensitive biosensor based on a new optical sensing system we developed was built for tracing fluorescence signal. The CQDs were immobilized on the end face of the optical fiber with a core diameter of 600 μm to fabricate sensing probe, and the sensor could detect NO quantitatively in real human serum samples with high sensitivity and fast real-time response.

Citric acid, o-phenylenediamine, acetone, sodium monohydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. S-Nitroso-N-Acetyl-DL-Penicillamine (SNAP) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. NO assay kit was purchased from NanJing JianCheng Bioengineering Institute. Human mixed serum was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-4500 Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs were measured using a UV-2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were performed on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The morphologies of the CQDs were achieved by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a model AXS D8-Advance automated diffractometer (Bruker, German). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were determined on an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα as the X-ray source for excitation (Thermo Fisher, USA). The morphology of the optical fiber probe was measured by atomic force microscope (VEECO, USA).

O-phenylenediamine (1.0 g) and citric acid (0.7 g) were heated and dissolved in 10 mL deionized water to obtain a clarified solution. Then the solution was placed in a 700 W microwave oven, taken out after microwave reaction for 3 min, naturally cooled to room temperature, and dissolved it with 10 mL deionized water to obtain CQDs solution. The solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min to remove any impurities and filtered by filter. The final CQDs solution was obtained after dialysis for 48 h using 3500 DA dialysis bag.

To examine the toxicity of the CQDs in living cells, SH-SY5Y cells were incubated in medium and allowed to incubate for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Medium containing different amounts of CQDs were separately added to the plates, and the plates were inserted into the incubator for 24 and 48 h. 20 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well, and continued to incubate for 2 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured with enzyme-labeled instrument, and finally the cell viability was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of optical fiber sensing membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose acetate (0.108 g) was added to acetone solution (3 mL), and after stirring for 2 h, 200 μL of the CQDs solution was added, followed by stirring for 6 h to obtain the film-forming solution. The film-forming solution was then fixed to the end face of the fiber by a process of pulling. The optical fiber was made of 10 cm long optical fiber with a core diameter of 600 μm. Before pulling, the optical fiber should be pretreated, and the coating of the optical fiber end face was stripped. The end face was sanded and smoothed, then washed with ethanol and dried.

The process of pulling method are as follows: the optical fiber was fixed on the lifting arm of the lifting machine, and the hanging optical fiber was immersed in the film-forming solution after immersing for 2 s. The dipping speed was raised at a speed of 120 mm/min, and the pulling times was 5 times. After the end of the pulling process, the optical fiber was removed from the lifting arm, and after standing 10 min for solvent acetone volatilization, the optical fiber sensing membrane was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of optical sensing system for nitric oxide detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the principle of optical fiber sensing, we designed and constructed the optical fiber NO sensing system. The schematic diagram of the sensing system is shown in Fig. 1 . The components of the sensing system mainly include 405 nm semiconductor laser ((MDL–III–405−100 mW, Changchun New Industry Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd), computer, optical power meter, attenuator, dichroic mirror, spectrometer (QE65000, Ocean Optics), 4× objective lens, and wavelength filter.

Firstly, the incident light is emitted from the laser source and enters the dichroic mirror placed at an angle of 45 degrees after passing the attenuator with adjustable incident light intensity. Light is divided into two parts, reflected light and transmitted light. The transmitted light is received by the probe of the optical power meter in order to control the incident light intensity in conjunction with the attenuator. The reflected light passes through the 4× objective lens and couples the light into the optical fiber sensing probe, and the light is transmitted along the core to the sensitive membrane on the end face of the optical fiber to generate excited fluorescence. The fluorescence signal is transmitted back along the optical fiber, passes through the objective lens, the dichroic mirror, and the wavelength filter, and is received by the spectrometer and processed by the computer to display the fluorescence spectrum.

The PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) was bubbled with N2 for 30 min to remove O2 from the solution and then used for the preparation of the nitric oxide solution. The NO standard solution was prepared from SNAP and PBS solution. SNAP is a nitric oxide releasing agent that can quantitatively release nitric oxide in solution and can control the concentration of nitric oxide between 1 × 10−12 and 1 × 10−3 mol/L [31,32].

The pre-processed optical fiber sensing probe was fixed on the optical sensing system. Then a small bottle containing NO standard solution of different concentration gradients (10∼100 nM, 10∼100 μM) was placed on the end of the optical fiber probe separately, and the calibration curve and formula were determined by calculating the fluorescence intensity changes before and after adding NO standard solution. The detection time was fixed at 5 min in order to stabilize the fluorescence of the fiber optic probe for reducing experimental errors. In addition, the power of the laser is set to 200 mW (10∼100 μM), 280 mW (10∼100 nM), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and properties of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of synthesized CQDs were 390 and 455 nm as shown in Fig. 2 (a), respectively. The UV–vis absorption peak near 244 nm was attributed to the π-π∗ transition of C-C in the framework of the CQDs, and the absorption peak at near 272 nm corresponded to n-π∗ transition of C = O and π-π∗ transition of C = C [33,34]. The X-ray diffraction pattern of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2(b). The synthesized CQDs had broad peaks at 28 and 44 degrees, which corresponded to the (002) and (100) crystal faces of graphite with a disordered layer structure, respectively [35].

In order to further explore the structure of CQDs, the surface morphology and particle size of CQDs were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). As shown in the Fig. 2(c), the CQDs were uniformly distributed and the particle size is about 5 nm.

The toxicity of fluorescent probe is an important consideration for the detection of NO concentration in human body. The cytotoxicity of the CQDs towards SH-SY5Y cells was determined using a CCK8 assay. The CQDs exhibited little cytotoxicity for cells as shown in Fig. 2(d). The cell viability still exceeded 85 % at high concentration (27.32 × 10−3 mg/mL) for 48 h incubation. The results showed that CQDs had good biocompatibility and can be used for the detection of NO in living cells.

We characterize the sensitive membrane of the fiber end face based on the pulling method in chapter 2.5. In Fig. 3 (a), the SEM photograph of the film on the end of optical fiber showed that there were many holes on the surface of the film, and the inside of each hole was loosely staggered. Therefore, the contact area with the detected solution can be greatly increased during the detection, thereby improving the sensitivity of the sensor.

In order to further explore the morphology of the fiber end face film, AFM was measured to observe the surface roughness of the film. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the surface of the film had a certain roughness and a relatively uniform degree of roughness, which increased the surface area of the film, thus greatly improving the sensitivity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of NO detection by the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence changes of the CQDs with a variety of NO concentrations range from 10 μM to 38 μM were measured in aqueous solution and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The fluorescent intensity of the CQDs decreased gradually with the increasing concentrations of the NO, indicating that CQDs had a good response to NO.

CQDs with NO recognition groups on the surface were prepared by microwave reaction for NO detection. The recognition group was composed of o-phenylenediamine group, which can increase the fluorescence of CQDs as an electron donor [36]. In the presence of NO, the reaction with o-phenylenediamine group produced an electron-free aryl triazole, which affected the fluorescence of CQDs. Based on this detection principle, the purpose of quantitative detection of NO was achieved. The reaction schematic is shown in Scheme1 .

In order to prove the reaction mechanism of NO quenching fluorescence of CQDs, the infrared spectra of CQDs with and without NO were compared. It can be seen from Fig. 5 (a) that both of them had absorption peaks at 742 cm−1, which corresponds to the bending and stretching vibration of NH with ortho-disubstituted benzene rings. Then CQDs without NO had absorption peaks at 3 386 cm−1, 3 363 cm−1 and 1 592 cm−1, which correspond to the symmetrical, anti-symmetrical and in-plane stretching vibrations of -NH2, respectively. The absorption peaks of the above three positions disappeared after adding NO, and then there was an absorption peak at 1 386 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN. Therefore, it can be explained that NO reacted with -NH2 on the o-phenylenediamine group with the surface of CQDs, resulting in the disappearance of -NH2.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis data of N1s provided additional information on reaction between CQDs and NO. The N1s data of CQDs is shown in Fig. 5(c). The peaks of binding energy at 399.8 eV and 401.4 eV correspond to -N-C- and Ph-NH2 (Ph: phenyl group), respectively. The N1s data of CQDs with NO is shown in Fig. 5(d). The peaks of binding energy at 397.9 eV, 398.6 eV and 399.8 eV ascribed to -N-H-, -N = N- in a cyclic structure and -N-C-, respectively [37,38]. Therefore, the formation of triazole groups on the surface of CQDs after the addition of NO further proved the above reaction mechanism.

The element content of CQDs before and after adding NO is shown in Table 1 . It can be seen intuitively that the element content of N increased significantly from 1.83 % to 14.54 % after adding NO. In addition, the content of O element also increased slightly, which may be caused by the reaction of a small amount of NO with O2 and H2O to produce NO2−.

The fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs was measured at optimum excitation wavelength with and without 5 × 10−6 mol/L NO aqueous solution. Before and after adding NO, the fluorescence decay curves of CQDs coincide, so their fluorescence lifetime values are the same. The decay of all samples was multi-exponential, and the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs calculated by using three exponentials was 1.903 ns. Fig. 5(b) shows the fluorescence decay curves of CQDs and CQDs added with quencher NO, indicating that the process of NO quenching CQDs was static quenching. The phenomenon of static quenching was caused by the formation of a non-luminescent ground state complex between the fluorescent substance and the quencher. The ground state complex formed competed with the ground state molecule of the fluorescent substance to absorb the excitation light, thereby reducing the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent substance. This further validated the mechanism above, and the reaction of NO with CQDs produced a ground state substance with a triazole group, thereby quenching the fluorescence of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration tests of the NO optic fiber sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching data of the CQDs were analyzed by Stern-Volmer equation [39,40]. The calibration method was as follows:
(1)
I 0
I
=
K
S V
Q
+ 1
where I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of the sensor without and with the quencher, respectively; [Q] is the quencher’s concentration; KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

The standard NO solution detection sequence chart was shown in the Fig. 6 (a). The optical fiber NO sensor was used in the aqueous medium. Therefore, the sensor should be calibrated firstly for avoiding the influence of the fiber sensing probe in different media. The specific method was to start putting the optical fiber probe on the optical platform, when the optical fiber probe was placed in the air medium, the fluorescence intensity tended to stabilize in 5 min. Then the fiber probe was inserted into the PBS buffer solution, and the fluorescence intensity value I0 was taken after it was stabilized. The fiber optic probe was pulled out to stabilize again in the air medium, and then the fiber probe was inserted into the standard NO solution, resulting the fluorescence intensity value I was taken after the fluorescence intensity tended to stabilize. Among them, when the fiber probe was removed from the PBS buffer solution, the fluorescence intensity could not return to the fluorescence intensity of the original fiber probe in the air medium for a period of time. The reason for this is that the optical fiber probe still contained certain amount of water due to the surface tension effect when it moved out. After a while, the water was volatilized due to the high energy of the laser, leading to the fluorescence being restored. From the sequence chart, it can be seen that the response time of the fiber optic sensor was very short. After each addition of NO, the sensor responded quickly and stabilized after 5 min.

The relationship between NO concentration and fluorescence intensity was clarified by calibration test. Fig. 6(b) and Fig. 6(c) show the relationship between the two concentration ranges of NO standard solution (10∼100 nM, 10∼100 μM) and I0/I, respectively. The calibration formula was determined by the Stern-Volmer’s equation. The calibration equation is I0/I = 0.00614[NO]+1 (R2 = 0.99972) in the range of NO concentration from 10 to 100 μM at laser power of 200 mW, I0/I = 0.00441[NO]+1 (R2 = 0.99988) in the range of NO concentration from 10 to 100 nM at laser power of 280 mW, and low detection limit of 9.12 nM.

The selectivity of the biosensor towards NO was investigated by separately adding substances, including NO2 −, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, and H2O2, to measure changes in fluorescence intensity ratio. As shown in Fig. 6(d), optical fiber sensor has higher specificity for detecting NO than other interferents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the sensor and NO assay kit
ce:section.ce:para: NO assay kit was adopted for comparative detection for exploring the detection accuracy of the sensors. Use of NO assay kits are considered to be relatively accurate in detecting NO concentration in vitro. NO is chemically active and rapidly converted to NO2 − and NO3 − in the body. The detection of NO by the assay kit utilized the specificity of nitrate reductase to convert NO3 − to NO2 −, and the concentration of NO was determined by the change of color depth [41].

Four concentrations of NO solution (20 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM, 80 μM) were measured by NO assay kit and the sensor respectively. The experimental results were shown in Table 2 . The concentrations detected by the biosensor were closer to the actual standard sample than that detected by the assay kit demonstrating that the CQD sensor had higher detection accuracy. The whole detection time of the NO assay kit needs about 4 h, while the biosensor only needs 5 min. Moreover, the detection process of the biosensors did not require strict temperature control to ensure the activity of the biological enzyme, thereby greatly simplifying the detection process while ensuring accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO in human serum
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the feasibility of optical fiber NO sensor for detection of NO in a biological system, the recovery experiment of NO in human serum was carried out by adding standard NO solution. The calculation formula of recovery rate is as follows:
(2)
R =
C M
−
C S
C
N O
×
100 %
where R is recovery rate; CM is detection concentration of serum mixed with NO solution; CS is original concentration of NO in serum; CNO is concentration of NO aqueous solution added. The experimental results are shown in the Table 3 . The average recovery rate of NO in human serum was 97.25 %, and the standard deviation was 4.89 %. Therefore, the optical fiber sensor had a better effect in detecting NO in human serum, and with potential to detect NO in actual clinical practice.

Combining the advantages of fluorescence analysis and optical fiber sensing, a real-time and highly sensitive fiber optic biosensor using the CQDs based on a new sensing system for the detection of NO was constructed successfully. The biosensor could detect the concentration of NO in aqueous medium with a wide range of NO concentrations from 1 × 10−8 to 1 × 10−4 mol⋅L−1, and the whole detection time and detection limit were 5 min and 9.12 nM at laser power of 280 mW, respectively. The NO fiber optic biosensor is expected to promote the development of NO detection and provide more help for the detection and prevention of some clinical diseases.

The feasibility and reliability of the proposed optical fiber biosensor for NO detection have been verified. However, in practical applications, the detection environment is more complex, we need to optimize the preparation of sensitive film and optical sensing platform to further improve the performance of the sensor.

Wei Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jun Huang: Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Liyun Ding: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Haitao Lin: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Sha Yu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Fang Yuan: Methodology, Resources, Data curation. Bing Liang: Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Financial Support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 51878524, NO. 61575150), Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province of China：2016GSF201066.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence-based detection of nitric oxide in aqueous and methanol media using a copper (II) complex

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mondal, P. Kumar, P. Ghosh, A. Kalita, Fluorescence-based detection of nitric oxide in aqueous and methanol media using a copper (II) complex, Chem. Commun. 47 (2011), 2964-2966.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric acoustogenic probe for in vivo imaging of endogenous nitric oxide

J. Macmicking, Q. Xie, C. Nathan, Nitric oxide and macrophage function, Annual Review of Immunology 15 (2003) 323-350.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitric oxide-releasing hyperbranched polyaminoglycosides for antibacterial therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: NO synthase and NO-dependent signal pathways in brain aging and neurodegenerative disorders: the role of oxidant/antioxidant balance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Calabrese, T. E. Bates, A. M. G. Stella, NO synthase and NO-dependent signal pathways in brain aging and neurodegenerative disorders: the role of oxidant/antioxidant balance, Neurochemical Research 25 (2000) 1315-1341.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitric oxide in inflammatory bowel disease: a universal messenger in an unsolved puzzle

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible light-controlled nitric oxide release from hindered nitrobenzene derivatives for specific modulation of mitochondrial dynamics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silk provides a new avenue for third generation biosensors: sensitive, selective and stable electrochemical detection of nitric oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. M. Musameh, C. J. Dunn, M. H. Uddin, T. D. Sutherland, T. D. Rapson, Silk provides a new avenue for third generation biosensors: sensitive, selective and stable electrochemical detection of nitric oxide, Biosensors and Bioelectronics 103 (2018) 26-31.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sonochemical and sustainable synthesis of graphene-gold (G-Au) nanocomposites for enzymeless and selective electrochemical detection of nitric oxide
